ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese crackling pork belly rice noodle soup; mapped to closest parent 'pork dish' class (FOODON_00002572) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pork meat dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for Vietnamese crackling pork belly noodle soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Vietnamese crackling pork belly rice noodle soup served in a Singapore hawker or restaurant context, typically weighing approximately 450 g and comprising broth, rice noodles, sliced crackling pork belly, fresh herbs, and bean sprouts.

nutrient_source.title: H2. Crackling Pork Belly 越南脆皮五花肉米粉 — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010798). Carbohydrate reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB record; this is noted as a likely data limitation given the dish contains rice noodles — see notes. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly data (USDA SR Legacy #10006) as a supplementary reference for saturated fat fraction.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Rice noodles (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB-reported carbohydrate for this dish is 0.0 g/100g, which is inconsistent with the dish's rice noodle component. GI of 65 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (bún / bee hoon) GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), where rice noodles have a GI of approximately 61–65. Glycemic load per serving is reported as 0.0 because the HPB source records 0 g carbohydrate/100g; actual GL is likely substantially higher given the rice noodle content — consumers and clinicians should treat the GL figure as unreliable pending corrected carbohydrate data from the vendor. GI category set to 'medium' reflecting the rice noodle component.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold — driven by fish sauce, soy sauce, and pork bone broth; potassium at only 47 mg/100g provides negligible offsetting effect.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced-sauce portions, avoid adding extra dipping sauces, and do not consume the broth, as the broth is the primary sodium vehicle.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0 g/100g is inconsistent with the dish's rice noodle (米粉) component, which is a high-GI refined carbohydrate; actual postprandial glucose impact is likely significantly higher than the nutrient data suggests.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice noodle portion and a larger herb and vegetable portion, verify actual carbohydrate content with the vendor, and monitor postprandial glucose carefully given the likely high glycemic load from the rice noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly is inherently high in saturated fat and cholesterol (89 mg/100g); the crackling preparation via deep-frying or high-heat roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids; sodium at 1050 mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combined burden of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, AGEs from high-heat preparation, and very high sodium; if consumed occasionally, choose a smaller portion of pork belly, discard the crackling skin, and avoid the broth.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is high in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); crackling preparation generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; sodium at 1050 mg/100g exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the combination of saturated fat, arachidonic acid, AGEs from high-heat frying, and very high sodium creates a strongly pro-inflammatory nutritional profile with no meaningful anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vietnamese Crackling Pork Belly Rice Noodle Soup (越南脆皮五花肉米粉, Bún Thịt Heo Quay Giòn) is a Vietnamese-origin noodle soup dish served widely in Singapore hawker centres and Vietnamese restaurants. The dish features sliced crackling pork belly — pork belly that has been marinated and then roasted or deep-fried at high heat to produce a crisp, blistered skin with a tender, fatty interior — served over thin rice noodles (bee hoon / bún) in a clear pork bone broth. The broth is simmered for several hours using pork bones, imparting a naturally sweet, savoury depth, and is seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, and aromatics. The bowl is typically garnished with fresh herbs (coriander, spring onion), bean sprouts, and a wedge of lime. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), but is very high in sodium (1050 mg/100g), making it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0 g/100g is inconsistent with the rice noodle component and likely reflects a data limitation; actual carbohydrate and glycemic load are expected to be substantially higher. The crackling preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for individuals with inflammatory conditions. This dish is not halal due to pork content. It is suitable for lunch and dinner contexts and is a popular item in Singapore's Vietnamese food segment.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010798, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data as a supplementary reference. The carbohydrate value of 0 g/100g is flagged as a likely HPB data limitation given the dish's rice noodle component, but the HPB record is otherwise treated as the authoritative source.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g and sugar_g as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. This is NOT naturally zero — the dish contains rice noodles (米粉 / bee hoon) which are a refined carbohydrate source. The 0.0 g/100g carbohydrate figure is a likely data limitation or reporting artefact in the HPB record, possibly reflecting only the pork belly component without the noodle component. Consumers and clinicians should treat the carbohydrate and sugar values as unreliable and verify with the vendor. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0 g/100g per HPB; this is plausible for a refined rice noodle and pork belly dish with minimal vegetable content, treated as confirmed zero per HPB. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): The following per_100g fields are set to -1.0 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010798 does not separately report these values, and no sufficiently close composite dish match was found in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA to provide these values with defensible confidence: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish entry and not available from USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish; polyunsaturated_fat_g — same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g; trans_fat_g — not reported by HPB and not determinable from available approved sources for this composite dish; vitamin_c_mg — vitamin profile not reported by HPB for G00010798 and no approved composite dish equivalent found in USDA FoodData Central; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_b6_mg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_b12_mcg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; folate_mcg_dfe — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_d_mcg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; vitamin_e_mg — same basis as vitamin_c_mg; phosphorus_mg — not reported by HPB for G00010798; zinc_mg — not reported by HPB for G00010798; selenium_mcg — not reported by HPB for G00010798; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for G00010798 and not available from an approved source for this specific composite dish. Sources consulted and confirming unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (G00010798), USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. GLYCEMIC INDEX NOTE: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 because the HPB source records 0 g carbohydrate/100g; actual GL is likely substantially higher. SATURATED FAT: 3.8 g/100g estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly (#10006) fat fraction data as a supplementary reference, since HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. DISH IDENTITY NOTE: The dish name 'H2. Crackling Pork Belly 越南脆皮五花肉米粉' is a menu-style identifier; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Vietnamese Crackling Pork Belly Rice Noodle Soup'. The dish is Vietnamese in origin and is served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for both Vietnamese (mandatory) and Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). ONTOLOGY NOTE: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' as no exact ontology code exists for this specific composite dish.
